seo.title: Insurance Code Generation AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for code generation AI agents in insurance. Covers State insurance regulations, NAIC Model Laws, GDPR/CCPA.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for code generation in insurance environments carries compounded risk: generated code may embed discriminatory logic into underwriting or claims systems, expose policyholder PII, or produce unexplainable decision pathways that violate NAIC Model Laws and state regulatory mandates. A single flawed code artifact pushed to production can trigger Fair Credit Reporting Act violations, class-action exposure, or license revocation across multiple states. This checklist ensures every stage of the LLM-driven code generation pipeline meets the strict compliance, auditability, and safety standards required in the insurance industry.

sections.heading: Pre-Generation Governance & Scope Controls
sections.description: Establish guardrails before the LLM agent generates any code, ensuring prompts and task definitions cannot produce discriminatory logic or unauthorized system access.

items.label: Define Prohibited Code Patterns Registry
items.description: Maintain a versioned registry of explicitly banned code patterns—such as use of race, gender, ZIP code as a proxy for protected class, or credit score inputs not permitted under FCRA—that the agent must never generate for underwriting or claims modules.

items.label: Restrict Agent Scope to Approved Insurance Domains
items.description: Configure the LLM agent with a domain allowlist (e.g., policy rating engines, claims intake forms, actuarial calculators) and block code generation requests targeting authentication systems, payment processors, or external data brokers without explicit approval.

items.label: Require Human-Authored Task Specifications for Underwriting Logic
items.description: Any code generation task touching underwriting decision logic must originate from a human-authored, compliance-reviewed specification document—not freeform user prompts—to prevent accidental introduction of discriminatory rating factors.

items.label: Validate Prompt Inputs for PII Injection
items.description: Scan all prompts submitted to the LLM agent for embedded policyholder PII (SSN, DOB, policy numbers) using regex and NER classifiers before forwarding to the model, rejecting and logging any prompt containing real customer data.

items.label: Establish Anti-Discrimination Variable Blocklist
items.description: Programmatically prevent the agent from accepting or generating code that references protected class variables (race, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age where prohibited) as defined by applicable state insurance anti-discrimination statutes.

items.label: Document Agent Authorization Chain
items.description: Record which human role (actuary, compliance officer, engineer) authorized each code generation task, storing this chain in an immutable audit log tied to the resulting code artifact for regulatory inspection readiness.

items.label: Set Maximum Complexity Thresholds for Generated Code
items.description: Define cyclomatic complexity and lines-of-code limits for agent-generated modules; flag outputs exceeding thresholds for mandatory human review before sandbox execution, reducing the risk of opaque logic in claims denial pathways.

sections.heading: Sandbox Execution & Code Safety Validation
sections.description: Ensure all LLM-generated code is executed, tested, and validated in an isolated environment before any integration with production insurance systems or policyholder data.

items.label: Enforce Network-Isolated Sandbox Execution
items.description: Run all agent-generated code in a containerized sandbox (e.g., gVisor, Firecracker) with no outbound network access, preventing exfiltration of policyholder data or unauthorized calls to external APIs during test execution.

items.label: Scan Generated Code for Credential and Secret Leakage
items.description: Before sandbox execution and after generation, run automated secret-scanning tools (e.g., Trufflehog, Gitleaks) against every generated file to detect hardcoded API keys, database credentials, or encryption keys that could expose insurance system access.

items.label: Perform Static Analysis for OWASP Top 10 Vulnerabilities
items.description: Run SAST tools (e.g., Semgrep with insurance-specific rulesets) on all generated code to detect SQL injection, insecure deserialization, and broken access control patterns before any execution, given that insurance databases contain highly sensitive policyholder records.

items.label: Validate Dependency Manifests Against Approved Package Registry
items.description: Any third-party library introduced by the agent must be checked against a pre-approved dependency allowlist and scanned for known CVEs using tools like Snyk or OWASP Dependency-Check before inclusion in the build pipeline.

items.label: Execute Syntax and Type Validation Before Runtime
items.description: Run language-specific linters and type checkers (e.g., mypy for Python, tsc for TypeScript) on all generated code as a mandatory pre-execution gate, preventing runtime failures in production claims or policy systems.

items.label: Enforce Resource Quotas in Sandbox Environment
items.description: Apply strict CPU, memory, and execution time limits to sandbox runs to prevent denial-of-service conditions and detect infinite loops or resource exhaustion bugs in generated actuarial computation code before production deployment.

items.label: Capture and Retain Full Sandbox Execution Logs
items.description: Store complete stdout, stderr, and system call traces from every sandbox execution for a minimum of 7 years in tamper-evident storage to support state regulatory audits and e-discovery in litigation scenarios.

items.label: Require Test Coverage Minimums for Insurance Business Logic
items.description: Mandate that agent-generated code for underwriting or claims modules achieves at least 85% unit test coverage, with tests explicitly covering edge cases for protected class inputs, before promotion out of sandbox.

sections.heading: Explainability, Auditability & Bias Detection
sections.description: Guarantee that all code generated for insurance decision systems produces explainable, auditable outputs and is tested for discriminatory bias before deployment, as required by NAIC Model Laws and state regulators.

items.label: Require Inline Explainability Comments for Decision Logic
items.description: Configure the LLM agent to generate mandatory inline documentation for every conditional branch in underwriting or claims denial code, explaining the actuarial or business rationale in plain language to satisfy state requirements for explainable AI decisions.

items.label: Run Disparate Impact Analysis on Generated Rating Algorithms
items.description: Before deployment, execute automated disparate impact tests on any agent-generated rating or scoring code using synthetic demographic datasets, flagging outputs where protected class proxies produce statistically significant differential outcomes (>80% rule threshold).

items.label: Generate Machine-Readable Audit Trails for Every Code Artifact
items.description: Automatically produce a structured audit record (JSON/XML) for each generated code artifact containing: prompt hash, model version, generation timestamp, sandbox test results, bias test outcomes, and approving human reviewer identity.

items.label: Map Generated Code to Regulatory Requirements
items.description: Maintain a traceability matrix linking each generated code module to the specific NAIC Model Law provision, state regulation, or internal compliance policy it implements or affects, enabling rapid impact assessment during regulatory examinations.

items.label: Implement Model Card Documentation for Agent-Generated Modules
items.description: Produce a standardized model card for each AI-generated decision module deployed to production, documenting intended use, known limitations, training data lineage (if applicable), and bias evaluation results for regulator and internal review.

items.label: Enable Real-Time Decision Logging for Claims Denial Code
items.description: Ensure all agent-generated claims processing code includes instrumentation to log every denial decision with the specific rule or factor triggered, storing logs in a queryable format to support the adverse action notice requirements under applicable state laws.

items.label: Schedule Periodic Retrospective Bias Audits on Deployed Code
items.description: Establish a quarterly review process where compliance teams analyze production outcomes from agent-generated underwriting and claims code using real (anonymized) decision data to detect emergent discriminatory patterns not caught in pre-deployment testing.

sections.heading: Data Protection, Access Control & Deployment Security
sections.description: Protect policyholder data throughout the code generation lifecycle and enforce strict access controls on generated artifacts, aligning with GDPR/CCPA, FCRA, and state insurance data security laws.

items.label: Prohibit Use of Real Policyholder Data in Code Generation Prompts
items.description: Enforce a technical control preventing any production database containing policyholder PII from being accessible to the code generation agent; all example data used in prompts must be synthetically generated and validated as non-identifiable.

items.label: Encrypt Generated Code Artifacts at Rest and in Transit
items.description: Store all LLM-generated code files in encrypted repositories (AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit) with access restricted to authorized engineering and compliance personnel, meeting the encryption requirements of NAIC Insurance Data Security Model Law.

items.label: Implement Role-Based Access Control on Code Generation Pipeline
items.description: Restrict who can initiate code generation tasks, approve sandbox results, and promote code to production using RBAC with separate roles for actuaries, compliance reviewers, and deployment engineers—no single role should have end-to-end unilateral access.

items.label: Audit-Log All Access to Generated Code Artifacts
items.description: Enable immutable access logging on all repositories storing agent-generated code, capturing user identity, timestamp, and action (read/write/delete) to support the access audit requirements under state insurance data security regulations and GDPR Article 30.

items.label: Enforce Code Signing Before Production Deployment
items.description: Require all agent-generated code modules to be cryptographically signed by an authorized compliance reviewer before deployment to production insurance systems, creating a non-repudiable record of human approval for regulatory purposes.

items.label: Implement Data Minimization in Generated Database Queries
items.description: Review and enforce that all agent-generated SQL or ORM queries follow data minimization principles—selecting only fields necessary for the specific insurance function—to comply with CCPA and GDPR data minimization requirements and limit breach exposure.

items.label: Define and Test Data Retention Policies in Generated Code
items.description: Validate that any agent-generated code handling policyholder data includes configurable retention and deletion logic aligned with state insurance record retention requirements (typically 5-7 years) and CCPA/GDPR right-to-erasure obligations.

items.label: Conduct Pre-Deployment Penetration Testing on Generated Modules
items.description: Before releasing any agent-generated module that processes policyholder data or underwriting decisions to production, perform targeted penetration testing focused on injection attacks, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration vectors specific to insurance system architectures.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: NAIC Model Laws (including the Insurance Data Security Model Law and Algorithmic Accountability in Insurance guidance) require insurers to maintain a comprehensive information security program covering all systems that process policyholder data—AI code generation pipelines that touch or produce code interacting with such data must be explicitly included in your Information Security Program documentation and annual board reporting.

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) imposes strict requirements on adverse action notices when credit information influences insurance decisions; any agent-generated code that incorporates credit-based insurance scores must include compliant adverse action notice generation logic, and the AI system itself must be disclosed as part of the decision process in applicable states.

State anti-discrimination statutes (including those in California, New York, Illinois, and others) prohibit the use of protected class characteristics—directly or through proxy variables—in underwriting and rating; generated code must be tested against each state's specific prohibited factor list, as these vary materially and a single national codebase may require state-specific compliance gates before deployment.

GDPR Article 22 and CCPA Section 1798.185 grant individuals rights related to solely automated decision-making; if agent-generated code produces automated underwriting or claims decisions affecting EU data subjects or California residents, you must implement documented human review mechanisms, provide meaningful information about the logic involved, and honor opt-out or contestation rights—failure to do so in generated code constitutes a regulatory violation regardless of the AI's role in authoring it.

cta.heading: Automate Your Insurance Agent Compliance
cta.description: Deploy Vex to continuously monitor your AI code generation pipeline against NAIC Model Laws, state insurance regulations, and anti-discrimination requirements—catching compliance gaps before they become regulatory actions.
